siteDescription: Free online regression equation calculator. Compute linear, polynomial, and multiple regression equations instantly with step-by-step solutions.

home.title: Regression Equation Calculator - Find Linear, Exponential & Polynomial Equations
home.description: Compute regression equations instantly with our free online tool
home.intro: Enter your data points and get the regression equation, slope, intercept, R-squared value, and step-by-step calculations in seconds.
home.whatIsText: A regression equation models the relationship between variables, allowing you to predict outcomes based on input data. In its simplest form — simple linear regression — the equation is y = mx + b, where m is the slope and b is the y-intercept. This powerful statistical tool is used across science, business, and engineering to uncover trends, make forecasts, and quantify relationships between variables.
whyUsePoints: Instantly computes the regression equation from your data
Shows step-by-step calculations so you understand the math
Calculates R², correlation coefficient, slope, and intercept
No signup required — 100% free and runs in your browser
Your data stays private — nothing is sent to any server
useCasesPoints: Estimating house prices from square footage
Analyzing the impact of study hours on exam scores
Evaluating the relationship between dosage and response
home.howItWorksStep1: Enter your x and y data points in the table below
home.howItWorksStep2: Click Calculate to compute the regression equation
home.howItWorksStep3: View your results including equation, slope, intercept, and R² value
home.tableDescription: Common regression statistics and their interpretations

about.title: About Us
about.description: Learn about the Regression Equation Calculator
about.content: The Regression Equation Calculator is a free online tool designed to help students, researchers, and professionals compute regression equations quickly and accurately. Our calculator supports linear regression and provides detailed step-by-step solutions to help you understand the mathematical process behind the results. Whether you're studying statistics, analyzing data, or working on research, our tool makes regression analysis accessible to everyone.
about.missionText: To make regression analysis accessible to everyone — from students learning statistics for the first time to professionals who need quick, reliable results. We believe that powerful statistical tools should be free, private, and easy to use.
about.valuesText: Privacy first — all calculations run in your browser, nothing is sent to any server. Education focused — every result includes step-by-step explanations so you learn the math, not just the answer. Always free — no signup, no paywall, no limits.
about.teamText: We are a small team of statisticians, educators, and developers passionate about making data analysis understandable. If you have suggestions or want to contribute, reach out through our contact page.

Use of the calculator is free and we do not guarantee the accuracy of results for professional or commercial purposes.

privacy.title: Privacy Policy
privacy.description: How we handle your data at the Regression Equation Calculator
privacy.content: We value your privacy. The Regression Equation Calculator does not collect, store, or share any personal data entered into the calculator. All calculations are performed locally in your browser. We may use analytics to understand site usage patterns, but no personally identifiable information is collected. Contact form submissions are processed securely and are not shared with third parties.

calculator.enterDataHint: Enter your data points below. For accurate regression analysis, include at least 10 data points covering a reasonable range.
calculator.resultHint: Your regression analysis results appear below. These calculations follow international statistical standards.
calculator.invalidData: Please enter at least 2 valid data points
calculator.predictionXHint: Enter a number to predict corresponding Y value
calculator.noteText: Results calculated using international statistical standards applicable in USA, Europe, Asia, and worldwide.

pearson.title: Pearson Correlation Calculator
pearson.description: Enter your data points to calculate the Pearson correlation coefficient
pearson.invalidData: Please enter at least 2 valid data points
pearson.causationWarning: Remember: Correlation does not imply causation. A significant correlation only indicates association, not that one variable causes the other.
pearson.whatIsTitle: What Is the Pearson Correlation Coefficient?
pearson.whatIsText: The Pearson correlation coefficient (r) measures the strength and direction of the linear relationship between two continuous variables. It ranges from −1 (perfect negative correlation) to +1 (perfect positive correlation), with 0 indicating no linear relationship. Unlike regression, which predicts one variable from another, correlation simply quantifies how closely two variables move together.
whenPoints: Both variables are continuous (numeric) and approximately normally distributed
The relationship appears linear (check with a scatter plot)
You want to measure association, not predict one variable from another
There are no extreme outliers that could distort the result
avoidPoints: The relationship is non-linear (use Spearman's rank correlation instead)
Your data has outliers (use Spearman or robust methods)
One or both variables are ordinal/categorical (use Spearman or chi-square)
You need to imply causation (correlation ≠ causation)

grubbs.title: Grubbs' Test Calculator
grubbs.description: Detect outliers in your dataset using Grubbs' test
grubbs.invalidData: Please enter at least 3 valid data points
grubbs.whatIsText: Grubbs' test (also called the maximum normed residual test) is a statistical procedure used to detect a single outlier in a univariate dataset that follows an approximately normal distribution. It tests the null hypothesis H₀ that there are no outliers, against H₁ that there is exactly one outlier. The test identifies the data point furthest from the mean, calculates the G statistic, and compares it to a critical value based on sample size and significance level.
howItWorksPoints: Calculate the sample mean (x̄) and standard deviation (s)
Find the observation furthest from the mean: max|xᵢ − x̄|
Compute the G statistic: G = max|xᵢ − x̄| / s
Compare G to the critical value from the t-distribution
If G > critical value, the extreme point is an outlier
assumptionsPoints: The data comes from a normally distributed population
Only one outlier may be present (test one at a time)
The test is applied to a single variable (univariate)
The sample size should be at least 3
whenPoints: You suspect a single outlier in normally distributed data
You want a formal statistical test rather than visual inspection
You need to decide whether to remove an extreme data point
You are quality-checking measurements for anomalies
avoidPoints: The data is not approximately normal (use Dixon's Q or robust methods)
Multiple outliers may exist (use Rosner's or Tietjen-Moore test)
The sample size is very small (n < 6, results are less reliable)
Your data is multivariate (use Mahalanobis distance instead)

assumptionsChecker.title: Regression Assumptions Checker
assumptionsChecker.description: Check all four linear regression assumptions for your data
assumptionsChecker.invalidData: Please enter at least 5 valid data points
assumptionsChecker.whatIsText: Linear regression makes four key assumptions about your data. When these assumptions are violated, your regression coefficients may be biased, p-values unreliable, and predictions inaccurate. Checking assumptions before trusting your model is a critical step that many analysts skip — leading to flawed conclusions.
assumptionsChecker.testsTitle: The Four Assumptions & How We Test Them
testsPoints: Linearity — The relationship between X and Y is linear. We test with Ramsey's RESET test (F-test on squared and cubed predicted values).
Independence — Residuals are not correlated with each other. We test with the Durbin-Watson statistic (d ≈ 2 means no autocorrelation).
Homoscedasticity — Residual variance is constant across predicted values. We test with the Breusch-Pagan test (LM statistic, chi-squared).
Normality — Residuals follow a normal distribution. We test with the Jarque-Bera test (skewness + kurtosis, chi-squared).
whenPoints: Before relying on regression results for decision-making
When your residual plots show suspicious patterns
After collecting new data for an existing model
When publishing research that uses regression analysis
assumptionsChecker.fixTitle: What to Do When Assumptions Fail
fixPoints: Non-linearity: Add polynomial terms (x²), apply transformations (log, sqrt), or use non-linear models
Autocorrelation: Add lag terms for time-series, use ARIMA or mixed-effects models
Heteroscedasticity: Transform y (log, sqrt), use weighted least squares, or robust standard errors
Non-normality: Transform y, remove outliers, or rely on CLT for large samples (n > 30)
assumptionsChecker.manualDemoTitle: How to calculate Pearson correlation by hand (step‑by‑step)
assumptionsChecker.manualDemoNote: This manual calculation helps you understand the correlation that underlies regression. Your regression assumptions (linearity, etc.) depend on this strength of relationship.

linearCalc.title: Linear Regression Calculator
linearCalc.description: Calculate the linear regression equation y = mx + b from your data
linearCalc.invalidData: Please enter at least 2 valid data points
linearCalc.whatIsText: Linear regression finds the best-fit straight line through your data points using the method of least squares. The equation takes the form y = mx + b, where m (slope) tells you how much y changes per unit increase in x, and b (intercept) is the predicted y-value when x = 0. The slope and intercept are chosen to minimize the sum of squared residuals — the differences between observed and predicted y-values.
linearCalc.formulaText:
Slope: m = Σ(xᵢ − x̄)(yᵢ − ȳ) / Σ(xᵢ − x̄)²
Intercept: b = ȳ − m·x̄
R²: 1 − SSres / SStot
These formulas use the normal equations for ordinary least squares (OLS). The denominator of the slope must be non-zero, meaning x cannot be constant.
linearCalc.exampleText: Suppose you have 5 data points: (1,2), (2,4), (3,5), (4,4), (5,5). The means are x̄ = 3.0 and ȳ = 4.0. Computing the sums of squares gives slope m = 0.6, intercept b = 2.2, and R² = 0.6. The regression equation is y = 0.6x + 2.2. This means y increases by 0.6 for each unit increase in x, and when x = 0 the predicted y is 2.2.
whenPoints: The relationship between x and y appears approximately linear (check with a scatter plot)
You want to predict a continuous outcome from one continuous predictor
Your residuals are roughly normally distributed with constant variance
You need a simple, interpretable model for trend analysis or forecasting
avoidPoints: The relationship is clearly curved — try our Quadratic Regression Calculator
You have multiple predictors — use our Multiple Regression Calculator
Your data has strong outliers that could distort the line
The variables are categorical or ordinal, not continuous
linearCalc.seeAlsoLinearDesc: Model curved relationships with y = ax² + bx + c
linearCalc.seeAlsoMultiDesc: Use two or more predictors with y = b₀ + b₁x₁ + b₂x₂
linearCalc.seeAlsoPearsonDesc: Measure the strength of the linear relationship
linearCalc.seeAlsoAssumptionsDesc: Verify that your data meets the four OLS assumptions

quadCalc.title: Quadratic Regression Calculator
quadCalc.description: Calculate the quadratic regression equation y = ax² + bx + c from your data
quadCalc.invalidData: Please enter at least 3 valid data points
quadCalc.whatIsText: Quadratic regression fits a parabola to your data using the equation y = ax² + bx + c. This is a form of polynomial regression (degree 2) that captures curved, U-shaped, or inverted-U relationships that a straight line cannot model. The coefficients are found by solving the normal equations — a system of three linear equations built from sums of powers of x and cross-products with y.
quadCalc.formulaText:
The coefficients a, b, c satisfy the normal equations:
nc + bΣx + aΣx² = Σy
cΣx + bΣx² + aΣx³ = Σxy
cΣx² + bΣx³ + aΣx⁴ = Σx²y
We solve this 3×3 system by Gaussian elimination to obtain a, b, and c.
quadCalc.exampleText: Suppose your data follows a parabolic trend: (−2,4), (−1,1), (0,0), (1,1), (2,4). This is exactly y = x². The calculator finds a = 1.0, b = 0.0, c = 0.0, giving the equation y = 1.0x² + 0.0x + 0.0 with R² = 1.0 (perfect fit). Real data won't be this clean, but quadratic regression excels whenever the scatter plot shows a clear curve.
whenPoints: A scatter plot shows a clear U-shaped or inverted-U pattern
Linear regression gives a poor R² and you see systematic curvature in residuals
The phenomenon has a natural peak or valley (e.g., performance vs. arousal, drug efficacy vs. dose)
You need a more flexible model but want to stay within the polynomial family
avoidPoints: The relationship is roughly linear — use our Linear Regression Calculator instead
You need more than one predictor — use our Multiple Regression Calculator
Higher-order curvature (cubic or beyond) is needed — consider degree-3+ polynomial regression
Extrapolating far beyond your data range — quadratics can diverge rapidly
quadCalc.seeAlsoLinearDesc: Model straight-line relationships with y = mx + b
quadCalc.seeAlsoMultiDesc: Use two or more predictors with y = b₀ + b₁x₁ + b₂x₂
quadCalc.seeAlsoPearsonDesc: Measure the strength of the linear relationship
quadCalc.seeAlsoAssumptionsDesc: Verify that your data meets the four OLS assumptions

multiCalc.title: Multiple Regression Calculator
multiCalc.description: Calculate the multiple regression equation from your data with two or more predictors
multiCalc.invalidData: Not enough data points for the selected number of predictors
multiCalc.whatIsText: Multiple regression extends linear regression to two or more predictors. The equation takes the form y = b₀ + b₁x₁ + b₂x₂ + …, where each coefficient bᵢ represents the effect of predictor xᵢ on y while holding all other predictors constant. This is the most widely used regression technique in research, business analytics, and machine learning because real-world outcomes usually depend on multiple factors simultaneously.
multiCalc.formulaText:
The coefficient vector β = (XᵀX)⁻¹Xᵀy, where:
X = design matrix (first column all 1s, then each predictor column)
y = vector of observed dependent variable values
β = vector of coefficients [b₀, b₁, b₂, …]
R² = 1 − SSres/SStot
Adjusted R² = 1 − (1−R²)(n−1)/(n−p−1), where p = number of predictors
Standard Error = √(SSres/(n−p−1))
multiCalc.exampleText: Imagine predicting house price (y) from square footage (x₁) and number of bedrooms (x₂). With 10 observations, the calculator might yield: y = 15,000 + 150·x₁ + 12,000·x₂. This means: each additional square foot adds ~$150 to the predicted price (holding bedrooms constant), and each additional bedroom adds ~$12,000 (holding sqft constant). R² = 0.89 means 89% of price variation is explained by the two predictors together.
whenPoints: Your outcome depends on more than one input variable simultaneously
You want to control for confounding variables (e.g., predict salary from both experience and education level)
You need to quantify each predictor's unique contribution to the outcome
Single-predictor linear regression leaves too much variance unexplained (low R²)
avoidPoints: You only have one predictor — use our Linear Regression Calculator
The relationship is curved — try our Quadratic Regression Calculator
Predictors are highly correlated with each other (multicollinearity) — consider dropping or combining variables
Your sample size is small relative to the number of predictors (need n > p + 1)
multiCalc.seeAlsoLinearDesc: Model straight-line relationships with y = mx + b
multiCalc.seeAlsoQuadDesc: Model curved relationships with y = ax² + bx + c
multiCalc.seeAlsoPearsonDesc: Measure the strength of the linear relationship
multiCalc.seeAlsoAssumptionsDesc: Verify that your data meets the four OLS assumptions
